summary:
The Cattle Period of Saharan rock art, dating approximately 5000–2500 BCE, represents one of the most extensive and vivid traditions of prehistoric visual art in the world, preserving images of the people and animals of the ancient Sahara when the region was far wetter and more hospitable than today. Thousands of rock paintings and engravings across the central Sahara — particularly in the Tassili n'Ajjer plateau of Algeria and the Acacus Mountains of Libya — depict cattle, humans, and pastoral scenes of exceptional artistic quality and detail.

The Cattle Period follows the earlier 'Round Head' phase of Saharan rock art and reflects a fundamental economic and cultural shift: the adoption of cattle herding by Saharan communities. The rock art depicts elaborate cattle, often shown with complex horn forms suggesting selective breeding, as well as pastoral scenes — herding, milking, and processing cattle products. The cattle herders of the ancient Sahara inhabited a landscape of lakes, rivers, and grasslands maintained by the African Humid Period (also called the 'Green Sahara'), when monsoon rainfall extended much further north than it does today.

The gradual desiccation of the Sahara after approximately 3000 BCE forced these pastoral communities southward and eastward, contributing to population movements that influenced the development of sub-Saharan African cultures and possibly contributing to the development of Predynastic Egyptian civilization through the introduction of cattle pastoralism into the Nile Valley. The rock art they left behind provides a priceless record of human life in a landscape that has since become one of the world's most inhospitable deserts.
